items.title: Zmanim Documentation
items.text:
This site explains common zmanim in plain language. Each page describes what a zman represents and, where available, the basic approach used to calculate it.

You may have reached this site because a zmanim app you use linked you here for more detail about a particular time. These pages are meant to help you understand the time shown in your app, including any sources, offsets, or calculation methods that may affect it.

Note that different apps may use different versions, settings, or defaults, so the information here should be used as a guide rather than a guarantee that every app calculates a zman in exactly the same way.

items.title: Alos 120 Minutes
items.text:
Deprecated because this is a very early time and should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day. Using it lekula can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, or dawn, calculated as a very early time that should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating by this time on a fast day, and not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated as 120 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting. It is based on the time to walk 5 mil at 24 minutes per mil, following the view of the Rishonim that the neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 5 mil.

The time depends on whether the elevation setting uses sunrise or sea-level sunrise.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Minutes

items.title: Alos 120 Zmanis
items.text:
Deprecated because this is a very early time and should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day. Using it lekula can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, or dawn, calculated as a very early zmaniyos-based time that should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating by this time on a fast day, and not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated as 120 zmaniyos minutes, or ⅙ of the day, before sunrise or sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting. It is based on a 24-minute mil, so 5 mil equals 120 minutes, which is ⅙ of a 12-hour day. The day is measured from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, or from sunrise to sunset depending on the elevation setting.

The time depends on whether the elevation setting uses sunrise and sunset or sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Zmanis

items.title: Alos 18 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, based on the sun’s position before sunrise.

Calculated when the sun is 18° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos18Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos18Degrees

items.title: Alos 19 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, based on the sun’s position before sunrise. This is the Rambam’s alos according to Rabbi Moshe Kosower’s Maaglei Tzedek, page 88, Ayeles Hashachar Vol. I, page 12, Yom Valayla Shel Torah, Ch. 34, p. 222, and Rabbi Yaakov Shakow’s Luach Ikvei Hayom.

Calculated when the sun is 19° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Degrees

items.title: Alos 19 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, based on the sun’s position before sunrise. It corresponds to a degree-based version of the 90-minutes-before-sunrise calculation.

Calculated when the sun is 19.8° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. This is based on the position of the sun 90 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which calculates to 19.8° below geometric zenith.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Point8Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Point8Degrees

items.title: Alos 26 Degrees
items.text:
Deprecated because this is a very early time and should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day. Using it lekula can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, or dawn, calculated as a very early time that should be used lechumra only, such as stopping eating by this time on a fast day, and not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated when the sun is 26° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. This is a degree-based version of the 120-minutes-before-sunrise calculation, based on the position of the sun 120 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which calculates to 26° below geometric zenith.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Alos 60 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, calculated as a fixed 60 minutes before sunrise. It is associated with the time to walk 4 mil at 15 minutes per mil.

Calculated as 60 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting. This fixed-offset approach is based on the view of the Rishonim that the neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

The time depends on whether the elevation setting uses sunrise or sea-level sunrise.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

The raw sources cited include the Chavas Yair, Chut Hashani Ch. 97, Divrei Malkiel Vol. 4, Ch. 20, page 34, Maharik Ch. 173, and the Ra'avan.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos60Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos60Minutes

items.title: Alos 72 Zmanis
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, calculated as 72 zmaniyos minutes, or 1/10 of the day, before sunrise. This calculation is used in calendars published by the Hisachdus Harabanim D'Artzos Habris Ve'Canada.

Calculated as 72 zmaniyos minutes before sunrise. It is based on an 18-minute mil, so 4 mil equals 72 minutes, which is 1/10 of a 12-hour day. The day is measured from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, or from sunrise to sunset depending on the elevation setting.

The time depends on whether the elevation setting uses sunrise and sunset or sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos72Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos72Zmanis

items.title: Alos 90 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, dawn, according to a fixed 90-minute interval before sunrise. This is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 22.5 minutes per mil, following the view of the Rishonim that the neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends only on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

Calculated as 90 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise, depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos90Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos90Minutes

items.title: Alos 90 Zmanis
items.text:
Alos, dawn, according to 90 zmaniyos minutes, equal to ⅛ of the day before sunrise. This is based on a 22.5-minute mil, so 4 mil equals 90 minutes, which is ⅛ of a 12-hour day.

Calculated as sunrise, or sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting, minus 1.5 GRA proportional hours.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Calculated as 96 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise, depending on the elevation setting.

Alos, dawn, according to 96 zmaniyos minutes, equal to 1/7.5 of the day before sunrise. This is based on a 24-minute mil, so 4 mil equals 96 minutes, which is 1/7.5 of a 12-hour day.

Calculated as sunrise, or sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting, minus 1.6 GRA proportional hours. The day is calculated from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, or from sunrise to sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Alos Baal Hatanya
items.text:
The Baal Hatanya’s alos, dawn, defined as the time when the sun is 16.9° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. It is based on 72 minutes between dawn and netz amiti, sunrise, the time to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil, associated with Rambam and others.

Calculated by the sun’s position: 16.9° below the geometric zenith. This angle corresponds to the sun’s position 72 minutes before netz amiti in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlosBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlosBaalHatanya

items.title: Alos 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, dawn, defined as the time when the sun is 16.1° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. It is based on 72 minutes between dawn and sunrise, the time to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil, associated with Rambam and others.

Calculated by the sun’s position: 16.1° below the geometric horizon. This angle corresponds to the sun’s position 72 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Alos 72 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, dawn, according to a fixed 72-minute interval before sunrise. This is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil. The 72-minute time is based on the view that the neshef, twilight between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

Calculated as 72 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise, depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Bein Hashmashos
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 13 Point 24 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos of Rabbeinu Tam, using a degree-based version of the 58.5-minutes-after-sunset calculation.

It is calculated when the sun is 13.24° below the western geometric horizon after sunset. This is based on the sun’s position 58.5 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which calculates to 13.24° below geometric zenith.

Yisrael Vehazmanim Vol. III page 1028, No. 50 says that a dip of slightly less than 13° should be used. The documentation notes 13.2456° as the proper dip, truncated to 13.24°, giving a time about 1.5 seconds earlier.

The difference between 13.24° and 13° is given as 1 minute 10 seconds in Jerusalem during the equinox, 1 minute 29 seconds in Jerusalem during the solstice, and 1 minute 56 seconds in New York during the solstice.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point24Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point24Degrees

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 13 Point 5 Minutes Before 7 Point 083 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos of Rabbeinu Tam according to this calculation.

It is calculated as 13.5 minutes, which is ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before shkiah calculated as 7.083°.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point5MinutesBefore7Point083Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point5MinutesBefore7Point083Degrees

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 2 Stars
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos of Rabbeinu Tam according to the opinion of the Divrei Yosef, as cited in Yisrael Vehazmanim.

It is calculated as 5/18, or 27.77%, of the time between alos calculated as 19.8° before sunrise and sunrise, added to sunset.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT2Stars

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT2Stars

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 58 Point 5 Minutes
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos of Rabbeinu Tam, calculated as a fixed offset after sunset.

It is calculated as 58.5 minutes after sunset. Bain hashmashos is ¾ of a mil before tzais, or 3 ¼ mil after sunset; with a mil calculated as 18 minutes, 3.25 × 18 equals 58.5 minutes.

This zman may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT58Point5Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT58Point5Minutes

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 13 Point 5 Minutes
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos, or twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais, or nightfall, starts at sunset.

It is calculated as 13.5 minutes, or ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before sunset.

This zman may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim13Point5Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim13Point5Minutes

items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais, or nightfall, starts at sunset.

It is calculated by the sun’s position 2.1° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux in Yerushalayim, corresponding to 13.5 minutes, or ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before sunset.

The beginning of bain hashmashos, or twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais, or nightfall, starts at sunset.

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 3 Point 05 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos, or twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais, or nightfall, starts at sunset.

Calculated when the sun is 3.05° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux, corresponding to 18 minutes, or ¾ of a 24-minute mil, before sunset.

A refraction value of 0.5166° is used lechumra, rather than the traditional 0.566°, resulting in a difference of about 14 seconds. The text says this is more in line with actual refraction in Eretz Yisrael and cites Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh, the Luach Itim Lebinah, and the article The Yereim's Bain Hashmashos.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz Baal Hatanya
items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Baal Hatanya.

Calculated as 5 hours into the day, with the day measured from netz amiti, sunrise, to sunset according to the Baal Hatanya.

This time is available only on Erev Pesach.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz MGA 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA).

Calculated as 5 shaos zmaniyos, solar hours, after dawn. In this opinion, the day is measured from dawn to nightfall, with both dawn and nightfall defined as when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon.

This time is available only on Erev Pesach.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzMGA16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzMGA16Point1Degrees

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz MGA 72 Minutes
items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA).

Calculated as 5 shaos zmaniyos, temporal hours, after dawn. In this opinion, the day is measured from dawn 72 minutes before sunrise to nightfall 72 minutes after sunset.

This time is available only on Erev Pesach.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanBiurChametzMGA72Minutes

items.text:
The latest time one is allowed to eat chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Baal Hatanya. This time is identical to Sof zman tfilah Baal Hatanya.

It is 4 Baal Hatanya shaos zmaniyos into the day, with the day calculated from netz amiti, sunrise, to sunset.

items.title: Sof Zman Shma 3 Hours Before Chatzos
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning, calculated as three regular clock hours before chatzos hayom. This is generally known as part of the “Komarno” zmanim after Rav Yitzchak Eizik of Komarno, though the source text says it predates him and lists earlier and later sources.

Three regular clock hours, not shaos zmaniyos, before chatzos hayom.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShma3HoursBeforeChatzos

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShma3HoursBeforeChatzos

items.title: Sof Zman Shma Alos 16 Point 1 To Sunset
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to the opinion that the day starts at alos when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon and ends at sea-level sunset. This is cited as the opinion of חידושי וכללות הרז״ה and מנורה הטהורה, as mentioned in Yisrael Vehazmanim vol. 1, sec. 7, ch. 3 no. 16.

Three shaos zmaniyos are calculated from a day that runs from alos 16.1° to sea-level sunset, and are added to alos.

Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.

The setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is ignored for this calculation.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShmaAlos16Point1ToSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShmaAlos16Point1ToSunset

items.title: Sof Zman Shma Alos 16 Point 1 To Tzais Geonim 7 Point 083 Degrees
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to the opinion that the day starts at alos when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon and ends at tzais when the sun is 7.083° below the horizon.

Three shaos zmaniyos are calculated from a day that runs from alos 16.1° to tzais 7.083°, and are added to alos.

Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.

The setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is ignored for this calculation.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Sof Zman Shma Ateret Torah
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning based on the calculation of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah.

The day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset, though that sunset offset is configurable. The time is three Ateret Torah shaos zmaniyos after alos 72 zmaniyos.

Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.

The time depends on the Ateret Torah sunset offset setting.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

items.title: Sof Zman Shma GRASunrise To Fixed Local Chatzos
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to Rav Moshe Feinstein’s calculation of the GRA opinion that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset, using only the first half of the day.

The half-day starts at sunrise and ends at fixed local chatzos. Sof zman Shema is three shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, which is half of this half-day.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Sof Zman Shma MGA 120 Minutes
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to the Magen Avraham (MGA), based on alos being 120 minutes, or ⅙th of the day, before sunrise. The source describes this as an extremely early zman and very much a chumra.

The day is calculated from dawn 120 minutes before sunrise to nightfall 120 minutes after sunset. The time is three shaos zmaniyos after that dawn.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.text:
The latest time to recite the morning prayers according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA), using a day calculated from dawn to nightfall with both at 19.8° below sunrise or sunset.

items.title: Kidush Levana
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sof Zman Kidush Levana 15 Days
items.text:
The latest time for Kiddush Levana according to the Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 426: 15 days after the molad.

Calculated as the moment 15 days after the molad. If that moment falls between alos and tzais, alos is used.

Some opinions understand the Rema, who cites the Maharil, as using the halfway-between-molados calculation, and as holding that the Mechaber agrees. The Aruch Hashulchan is also referenced on this subject.

For additional details, see Rabbi Dovid Heber’s discussion in Siman Daled, chapter 4, of Shaarei Zmanim.

This time is given even if it occurs during the day. A separate night-limited version limits the time to between tzais and alos.

On days when this zman does not occur, no time is shown.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days

items.title: Sof Zman Kidush Levana Between Moldos
items.text:
The latest time for Kiddush Levana according to the Maharil, who calculates it as halfway between one molad and the next.

Calculated by adding half of the 29 days, 12 hours, and 793 chalakim between molados to the month’s molad. This half interval is 14 days, 18 hours, 22 minutes, and 666 milliseconds. If that moment falls between alos and tzais, alos is used.

This time is given even if it occurs during the day. A separate night-limited version limits the time to between tzais and alos.

On days when this zman does not occur, no time is shown.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos

items.title: Tchilas Zman Kidush Levana 3 Days
items.text:
The earliest time for Kiddush Levana according to Rabbeinu Yonah, who says it can be said 3 days after the molad.

Calculated as the moment 3 days after the molad.

This time is given even if it occurs during the day, when Kiddush Levana cannot be said. A separate night-limited version limits the time to night hours.

On days when this zman does not occur, no time is shown.

items.title: Tchilas Zman Kidush Levana 7 Days
items.text:
The earliest time for Kiddush Levana according to the opinions that it should not be said until 7 days after the molad.

Calculated as the moment 7 days after the molad, whether it is day or night.

This time is given even if it occurs during the day, when Kiddush Levana cannot be recited. A separate night-limited version limits the time to night hours.

items.title: Chatzos Hayom
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sun Transit
items.text:
Sundial or solar noon, when the Sun is transiting the celestial meridian. See The Definition of Chatzos for details on the proper definition of solar noon or midday.

The calculation depends on the astronomical calculator being used. With the default NOAA calculator, it calculates astronomical noon. With the SunTimes calculator, which does not calculate astronomical noon directly, it is calculated as halfway between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset.

When calculated as halfway between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, the time can be slightly off the real transit time because of changes in declination, meaning the lengthening or shortening of the day.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as with the USNO calculator in the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunTransit

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunTransit

items.title: Fixed Local Chatzos Hayom
items.text:
Fixed local chatzos is a form of noon adjusted from standard time to account for the local longitude. It is described as the time of chatzos according to the Aruch Hashulchan in Orach Chaim 233:14 and Rabbi Moshe Feinstein in Igros Moshe Orach Chaim 1:24 and 2:20.

The globe’s 360° divided by 24 hours gives 15° per hour, or 4 minutes per degree. At longitudes 0°, 15°, 30°, and so on, chatzos is exactly 12:00 noon. Other longitudes are adjusted by their distance from the closest multiple of 15°, at 4 minutes per degree.

For example, Lakewood, New Jersey, at longitude -74.222, is 0.778° away from the closest multiple of 15° at -75°. Multiplying by 4 gives an adjustment of 3 minutes and 7 seconds, making chatzos 11:56:53.

This calculation is not tied to theoretical 15° time zones. It adjusts to the actual time zone and Daylight saving time.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getFixedLocalChatzosHayom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getFixedLocalChatzosHayom

items.title: Chatzos Hayom
items.text:
Chatzos hayom is determined either as astronomical chatzos or as the halfway point between sunrise and sunset, depending on the supported calculation and configuration. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of the ways to calculate chatzos.

When astronomical chatzos is supported and enabled, it uses astronomical chatzos. Otherwise, it uses the halfway point between sunrise and sunset. The USNO calculator calculates chatzos as halfway between sunrise and sunset, identical to six shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, while the NOAA calculator calculates it more accurately as astronomical chatzos.

In the Arctic, half-day chatzos may not be available on a day when either sunrise or sunset did not happen. If that happens and astronomical chatzos is supported, astronomical chatzos is used instead.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set, and the calculation being used does not support astronomical chatzos.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayom

items.title: Chatzos Hayom As Half Day
items.text:
Chatzos calculated as halfway between sunrise and sunset. Many are of the opinion that chatzos is calculated as the midpoint between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, despite this not being the most accurate way to calculate it. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of the ways to calculate chatzos.

It is calculated as the midpoint between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, equivalent to six shaos zmaniyos after sunrise. A day starting at alos and ending at tzais using the same time or degree offset will also produce the same time.

Because of the lengthening or shortening of the day, this is not necessarily the exact midpoint of the day in reality, but it is very close.

The SunTimes calculator calculates chatzos as halfway between sunrise and sunset, while the NOAA calculator calculates astronomical chatzos, which is described as slightly more accurate.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay

items.title: Midnight
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Solar Midnight
items.text:
Solar midnight is the end-of-day solar midnight for the date being calculated, which may actually be after civil midnight. For example, calculating it for February 8 means the midnight between February 8 and February 9. It occurs when the Sun is transiting the lower celestial meridian, or when the Sun is at its nadir.

The calculation depends on the astronomical calculator being used. With the default NOAA calculator, it calculates astronomical midnight. With the USNO calculator, which does not calculate astronomical noon, midnight is calculated as 12 hours after the halfway point between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset for that day.

The USNO-based calculation can be slightly off the real transit time because of changes in declination, meaning the lengthening or shortening of the day.

See The Definition of Chatzos for details on the definition of solar noon or midday.

The time may not be available when using the USNO calculator in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the Sun does not rise and one day when it does not set. This is not expected when using the NOAA calculator.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSolarMidnight

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSolarMidnight

items.title: Chatzos Halayla
items.text:
Chatzos halayla is calculated for the end of the day, as the last zman of the day, and may actually be after civil midnight. For example, when calculating it for Erev Pesach, it is calculated for Lail Pesach so it can be used as sof zman achilas chametz.

If astronomical chatzos halayla is supported and the setting to use astronomical chatzos is enabled, astronomical chatzos halayla is used. Otherwise, it is calculated as the halfway point between sunset and the following day’s sunrise. The USNO calculator uses the halfway point between sunset and the following day’s sunrise, identical to six shaos zmaniyos after sunset, while the NOAA calculator calculates it more accurately as astronomical chatzos halayla.

See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of the ways to calculate chatzos.

The halfway-between-sunset-and-sunrise calculation may not be available in the Arctic on a day when either sunset or the following day’s sunrise did not happen. If that calculation is unavailable and astronomical chatzos is supported, astronomical chatzos is used instead.

The time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day when the Sun does not rise and one day when it does not set, if astronomical calculations are not supported.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHalayla

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHalayla

items.title: Mincha Gedola
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Mincha Gedola 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Magen Avraham, with the day starting and ending when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. This is the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos, using a day based on alos at 16.1° below the horizon. If astronomical chatzos is used for other zmanim, it is calculated as half of a half-day shaah zmanis after chatzos, based on the period between chatzos and alos at 16.1° below the horizon.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in northern or southern locations where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola16Point1Degrees

items.title: Mincha Gedola 30 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha gedola calculated as 30 regular minutes after chatzos. Some use this time to delay the start of mincha in the winter when half of a shaah zmanis is less than 30 minutes. One should not use this time to start mincha before the standard mincha gedola. See Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim 234:1 and the Shaar Hatziyon seif katan ches.

Calculated as 30 regular clock minutes after chatzos, rather than as half of a shaah zmanis after chatzos.

Because this is a fixed 30 minutes after chatzos, it is affected by the setting for astronomical chatzos, not by the setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola30Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola30Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola 72 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Magen Avraham, with the day starting 72 minutes before sunrise and ending 72 minutes after sunset. This is the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos, using shaah zmanis based on a day from 72 minutes before sunrise to 72 minutes after sunset. If astronomical chatzos is used for other zmanim, it is calculated as half of a half-day shaah zmanis after chatzos, based on the period between chatzos and tzais 72 minutes.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola72Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola Ahavat Shalom
items.text:
Mincha gedola based on the opinion of Rabbi Yaakov Moshe Hillel, as published in the luach of the Bais Horaah of Yeshivat Chevrat Ahavat Shalom. Mincha gedola is the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as half a shaah zmanis after chatzos, with shaos zmaniyos based on a day starting at alos 16.1° and ending at tzais 3.7°, 13.5 minutes after sunset. The later of this time or 30 regular minutes after chatzos is used.

The setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim does not apply here, because this calculation involves chatzos offset from the center of the astronomical day.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in northern or southern locations where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAhavatShalom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAhavatShalom

items.title: Mincha Gedola Ateret Torah
items.text:
Mincha gedola based on the calculation of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah. This is the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the opinion of the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos. The day starts 1/10 of the day before sunrise and is usually calculated as ending 40 minutes after sunset, though that sunset offset can be configured to another value.

This time may depend on the configured Ateret Torah sunset offset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAteretTorah

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAteretTorah

items.title: Mincha Gedola Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Baal Hatanya. Mincha gedola is the earliest time one can pray mincha. The Rambam is of the opinion that it is better to delay mincha until mincha ketana, while the Ra"sh, Tur, GRA, and others are of the opinion that mincha can be prayed lechatchila starting at mincha gedola.

Calculated as 6.5 sea-level solar hours after netz amiti, or sunrise, based on the Baal Hatanya’s view that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRAFixed Local Chatzos 30 Minutes
items.text:
Rav Moshe Feinstein’s opinion for calculating mincha gedola, the earliest time one can pray mincha according to the GRA.

Calculated as 30 minutes after fixed local chatzos.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAFixedLocalChatzos30Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAFixedLocalChatzos30Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRAGreater Than 30
items.text:
The later of standard GRA mincha gedola and mincha gedola calculated as 30 regular minutes after chatzos. In the winter, when half of a shaah zmanis is less than 30 minutes, the 30-minute-after-chatzos time is used; otherwise, the standard GRA mincha gedola is used.

Calculated by comparing standard GRA mincha gedola with 30 minutes after chatzos and using whichever is later.

Because this calculation can be an offset from chatzos when 30 regular minutes is greater than half of a shaah zmanis, it may be affected by the setting for astronomical chatzos even when the setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is not used.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAGreaterThan30

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAGreaterThan30

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRA
items.text:
Mincha gedola is the earliest time one can pray mincha. This time follows the GRA. The Ramba"m is of the opinion that it is better to delay mincha until mincha ketana GRA, while the Ra"sh, Tur, GRA, and others are of the opinion that mincha can be prayed lechatchila starting at mincha gedola.

It is calculated as 6.5 shaos zmaniyos, or solar hours, after sunrise. The day is measured from sunrise to sunset, using either sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset or elevation-adjusted sunrise and sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

The time depends on whether elevation is used.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRA

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRA

items.title: Mincha Ketana
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Mincha Ketana 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to the Magen Avraham, with the day defined as starting and ending when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos, using a solar hour based on alos at 16.1° and the corresponding end of the day at 16.1° below the horizon.

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

items.title: Mincha Ketana 72 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to the Magen Avraham, with the day defined as starting 72 minutes before sunrise and ending 72 minutes after sunset. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos, using a solar hour based on a day from alos 72 minutes before sunrise to the end of the day 72 minutes after sunset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana72Minutes

items.title: Mincha Ketana Ahavat Shalom
items.text:
Mincha ketana based on the opinion of Rabbi Yaakov Moshe Hillel, as published in the luach of the Bais Horaah of Yeshivat Chevrat Ahavat Shalom. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 2.5 shaos zmaniyos before tzais at 3.8°, with shaos zmaniyos based on a day that starts at alos 16.1° and ends at tzais 3.8°.

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaAhavatShalom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaAhavatShalom

items.title: Mincha Ketana Ateret Torah
items.text:
Mincha ketana based on the calculation of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos. In this calculation, the day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset.

The end of the day is usually 40 minutes after sunset, but that offset is configurable to any offset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaAteretTorah

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaAteretTorah

items.title: Mincha Ketana Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to the Baal Hatanya. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 sea-level solar hours after netz amiti, sunrise. This follows the Baal Hatanya’s view that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Samuch Le Mincha Ketana 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Samuch lemincha ketana means near mincha ketana. It is described as the time when eating or other activity cannot begin before praying mincha. See the Mechaber and Mishna Berurah 232 and 249:2.

Calculated as half an hour before mincha ketana, or as 9 shaos zmaniyos after the start of the day. For this version, the day is based on alos 16.1° and tzais 16.1°, and the calculation used is 9 solar hours after alos 16.1°.

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Samuch Le Mincha Ketana GRA
items.text:
Samuch lemincha ketana is the time near mincha ketana, half an hour before mincha ketana. It is described as the time before which eating or other activity cannot begin before praying mincha.

It is calculated as 9 shaos zmaniyos, or solar hours, after sunrise, according to the GRA, using a day that starts at sunrise and ends at sunset. The sunrise used may be regular sunrise or elevation-adjusted sunrise, depending on the elevation setting.

The source references the Mechaber and Mishna Berurah 232 and 249:2.

This time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in some northern or southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.text:

It is calculated as 9.5 shaos zmaniyos, or solar hours, after sunrise, according to the GRA. The day is measured either from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset or from sunrise to sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Misheyakir 12 Point 85 Degrees
items.text:
Deprecated because it is a very early misheyakir time that should only be used bish’as hadchak. Lechatchila, a later zman should be used.

A very early misheyakir time mentioned for use bish’as hadchak, using the point when the sun is 12.85° below geometric zenith.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 12.85° below geometric zenith, based on the sun’s position slightly later than 57 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

This zman is mentioned for use bish’as hadchak in the Birur Halacha Tinyana and Tlisa’ah in Orach Chaim siman 18 as 12.85°.

Actual calculations show the 57-minute time to be slightly more than 12.9°, while 12.85° is described as a slight chumra on a bedieved time, with a difference of about 14 seconds at the equinox/equilux in Jerusalem.

The 57-minute based time is mentioned by the Minchas Yitzchak vol. 9, siman 9 as 15 minutes before alos hashachar, though the location is not clear and no degree-based conversion is mentioned.

The Kaf Hachaim vol. 1 siman 18, no. 18 states that in Yerushalayim 60 fixed minutes are used year round. Calculations show that 60 fixed minutes in Yerushalayim ranges from 13.5° at the spring equinox to 11.5° at the summer solstice.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees

items.title: Misheyakir 7 Point 65 Degrees
items.text:
Misheyakir based on a 35/36-minute zman around the equinox / equilux, using the point when the sun is 7.65° below geometric zenith.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 7.65° below geometric zenith. The degree value is based on a 35/36-minute zman around the equinox / equilux, when the neshef is shortest.

This time is based on Rabbi Moshe Feinstein, who writes in Ohr Hachaim Vol. 4, Ch. 6 that misheyakir in New York is 35–40 minutes before sunrise, something that is a drop less than 8°.

Rabbi Yisroel Taplin in Zmanei Yisrael notes that Rabbi Yaakov Kamenetsky stated that it is not less than 36 minutes before sunrise, and maybe it is 40 minutes.

The source states that this is also the accepted minhag in Lakewood used in the Yeshiva.

Since this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to it.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees

items.title: Misheyakir 9 Point 5 Degrees
items.text:
Misheyakir based on a 45-minute calculation in Baltimore, using the point when the sun is 9.5° below geometric zenith.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 9.5° below geometric zenith. This is based on Rabbi Dovid Kronglass’s calculation of 45 minutes in Baltimore, mentioned in Divrei Chachamim No. 24 and brought by the Birur Halacha, Tinyana, Ch. 18.

The source also cites Rabbi Yaakov Yitzchok Neiman in Kovetz Eitz Chaim Vol. 9, p. 202, that the Vya’an Yosef did not want to rely on times earlier than 45 minutes in New York.

This zman is also used in calendars published by Rabbi Hershel Edelstein and by Edot Hamizrach communities.

This also follows the opinion of Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who provided times of 36 and 45 minutes but did not provide a degree-based time. Since this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees

items.text:
The time of plag hamincha according to Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah.

The day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset, with that sunset offset configurable. Shaos zmaniyos are calculated from this day, and plag hamincha is 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after dawn.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.text:

It is calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise. The day is calculated from sunrise to sunset, or from sea level sunrise to sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

The time depends on whether the elevation setting is used.

The time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Sea Level Sunrise
items.text:
Sunrise calculated at sea level, without an elevation adjustment.

This is the sea-level sunrise time and serves as the base for dawn calculations that are calculated as a dip below the horizon before sunrise.

Non-sunrise and sunset calculations such as dawn and dusk depend on visible light, which is not affected by elevation.

The time may not be available if it cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunrise

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunrise

items.title: Sunrise
items.text:
Sunrise adjusted for elevation.

The calculation uses a geometric zenith of 90° plus an elevation adjustment. It is also adjusted by approximately 50/60 of a degree to account for 34 arcminutes of refraction and 16 arcminutes for the sun’s radius, for a total of 90.83333°.

The exact calculation may depend on the specific astronomical calculator being used.

The time may not be available if it cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunrise

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunrise

items.title: Sunrise Or Easternmost Solar Azimuth
items.text:
Sunrise if it occurs that day; otherwise, the time when the sun is at its easternmost position, an azimuth of 90°.

In Polar regions, where there can be days without sunrise or sunset, this follows opinions that during these periods the day-night boundary is when the sun is at its westernmost position, with the corresponding sunrise-side time at azimuth 90°. This is the opinion of Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz in דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש and דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳, and is brought down lehalacha by the Ben Ish Chai in רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳.

This time is close to six hours before astronomical chatzos hayom, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic or Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after that time.

If there is no sunrise that day and the sun also does not reach azimuth 90°, the time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth

items.title: Sea Level Sunset
items.text:
Sunset calculated as if the location were at sea level, without any elevation adjustment. It is used as the base for dusk calculations that are based on the sun dipping below the horizon after sunset.

Calculated at sea level, without an elevation adjustment.

Non-sunrise and sunset calculations such as dawn and dusk depend on visible light, which is not affected by elevation.

This time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunset

items.title: Sunset
items.text:
The exact sunset time, adjusted for elevation.

Calculated using a geometric zenith of 90° plus an elevation adjustment. The calculation is also adjusted by approximately 50/60 of a degree, accounting for 34 arcminutes of refraction and 16 arcminutes for the sun’s radius, for a total of 90.83333°.

In certain cases, the calculated sunset can occur before sunrise. This typically happens when a time zone other than the local time zone is used; in that case, the sunset date is moved to the following date.

This time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunset

items.title: Sunset Or Westernmost Solar Azimuth
items.text:
Sunset, when sunset occurs. If sunset does not occur that day, this is the time when the sun is at its westernmost position, at azimuth 270°.

If sunset occurs, it uses sunset. If sunset does not occur, it uses the time the sun reaches azimuth 270°. This time is close to six hours after astronomical chatzos hayom, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic or Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after that time.

In Polar regions, the Arctic or Antarctic circles, there are days with no sunrise or sunset. There are opinions that during these periods, the day-night boundary is when the sun is at its westernmost position.

In this opinion, sunrise is when the sun is at azimuth 90°, its easternmost position.

This is the opinion of Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz in דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש and דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳. This is brought down lehalacha by the Ben Ish Chai in רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳.

If there is no sunset that day and azimuth 270° also does not occur, this time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth

items.title: Tzais 120 Minutes
items.text:
Deprecated as a warning that this very late time should be used only lechumra; using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos, etc.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to Rav Chaim Naeh, based on a very late calculation that should be used only lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.

Calculated as 120 minutes after sunset or sea-level sunset, depending on the elevation setting. This is based on the view that a mil according to the Rambam is 24 minutes, and on the opinion of Ula that tzais is 5 mil after sunset.

The time depends on whether elevation is used: it is counted from sunset or from sea-level sunset.

This zman is extremely late and long past the 18° point where the darkest point is reached.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais120Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais120Minutes

items.title: Tzais 120 Zmanis
items.text:
Deprecated as a warning that this very late time should be used only lechumra; using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos, etc.

Tzais, or dusk, using a very late calculation of 120 minutes zmaniyos after sea-level sunset. It should be used only lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.

Calculated as 120 minutes zmaniyos after sea-level sunset.

This zman is extremely late and, in most places on the globe, occurs when the sun is well below the 18° point, described here as the scientifically darkest point.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais120Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais120Zmanis

items.title: Tzais 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais at the point when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon.

This degree is described as the sun’s dip below the horizon 72 minutes after sunset according to Rabbeinu Tam’s calculation of tzais around the equinox / equilux in Jerusalem.

The question of equinox versus equilux is described as complex. Rabbi Meir Posen in the Ohr Meir is cited as favoring the equilux, while Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh and Rabbi Yonah Mertzbuch are cited as favoring the astronomical equinox. The difference is about 9 seconds.

The time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais16Point1Degrees

items.text:
Deprecated as a warning that this very late time should be used only lechumra; using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos, etc.

Tzais based on when the sun is 26° below the horizon. This is an extremely late time and should be used only lechumra, such as delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.

Calculated by the sun reaching 26° below the horizon.

This zman is long past the 18° point where the darkest point is reached.

items.title: Tzais 50 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of Rabbi Moshe Feinstein for the New York area.

Calculated as 50 minutes after sunset or sea-level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

The time depends on whether elevation is used: it is counted from sunset or from sea-level sunset.

This time should not be used for latitudes other than ones similar to the latitude of the New York area.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais 60 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of the Chavas Yair and Divrei Malkiel that the time to walk a mil is 15 minutes.

Calculated as 60 minutes, or 4 mil, after sunset or sea-level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

The time depends on whether elevation is used: it is counted from sunset or from sea-level sunset.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais 72 Zmanis
items.text:
Tzais, or dusk, according to this calculation is the Minchas Cohen’s calculation of Rebbeinu Tam’s time of tzeis.

It is calculated as 72 proportional minutes, equal to 1/10 of the day, after sea level sunset.

This calculation makes the time from sunset to tzais shortest at the winter solstice, longest at the summer solstice, and 72 clock minutes at the equinox.

The source notes that this does not match reality, because there is no direct relationship between day length and twilight. It says the shortest twilight is during the equinox, the longest is during the summer solstice, and winter twilight is longer than during the equinoxes despite winter having the shortest daylight.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais72Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais72Zmanis

items.title: Tzais 90 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the Magen Avraham’s opinion that the time to walk a mil according to the Rambam is 18 minutes, with tzais calculated as 5 mil after sunset.

It is calculated as 90 minutes after sunset or sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

A similar calculation uses solar-position calculations based on this time.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais90Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais90Minutes

items.title: Tzais 90 Zmanis
items.text:
Tzais, or dusk, known in Yiddish as the achtel zman, an eighth zman, used in various kehilos.

It is calculated as 90 proportional minutes, equal to ⅛ of the day, after sea level sunset.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais90Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais90Zmanis

items.title: Tzais 96 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or dusk, calculated as a fixed 96 minutes after sunset.

It is calculated as 96 minutes after sunset or sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais 96 Zmanis
items.text:
Tzais, or dusk, calculated using proportional minutes.

It is calculated as 96 proportional minutes, equal to 1/7.5 of the day, after sea level sunset.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais Ateret Torah
items.text:
Tzais associated with Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah, who uses this time only for calculating other zmanim such as Sof Zman Krias Shema and Plag Hamincha. His calendars do not publish a zman for Tzais.

It is usually calculated as 40 minutes after sea level sunset, with the offset configurable to another number of minutes.

Chacham Harari-Raful provided a 25-minute zman for Israel.

The default is 40 minutes year-round in any place on the globe.

The time depends on the Ateret Torah sunset offset setting.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisAteretTorah

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisAteretTorah

items.title: Tzais Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya.

It is calculated when the sun is 6° below the western geometric horizon after sunset. This is based on the sun’s position about 24 minutes after sea level sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, 6° below geometric zenith. The calculation described by About Our Zmanim Calculations @ Chabad.org starts from shkiah amitis as 1.583° below the horizon, around the equinox / equilux, which is 3.516 minutes after sunset, then adds 18 minutes and two minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi. This comes to the sun being 5.83° below the horizon and is rounded up to 6°.

The source notes that this is very close to a slightly later calculation of tzais at 5.95° below the horizon that was based on 4 fixed minutes.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisBaalHatanya

items.title: Tzais 72 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on Rabbeinu Tam’s opinion that tzeis hakochavim is 72 minutes after sunset, the time it takes to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil.

It is calculated as 72 standard clock minutes after sunset or sea level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

According to the Machtzis Hashekel in Orach Chaim 235:3, the Pri Megadim in Orach Chaim 261:2, and others, the 72 minutes are standard clock minutes any time of year in any location.

The time may not be available or cannot be calculated in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getTzais72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getTzais72Minutes

items.title: Tzais Geonim 3 Point 7 Degrees
items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, defined by the sun being 3.7° below geometric zenith.

This corresponds to the sun’s position 13.5 minutes after sunset, based on the time to walk ¾ of a mil at 18 minutes per mil. In Jerusalem, the sun is 3.7° below geometric zenith around the equinox / equilux at this time.

This does not include the 26.46 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of an 18-minute mil.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh lists 3.65°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point7Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point7Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 3 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, defined by the sun being 3.8° below geometric zenith.

This corresponds to the sun’s position 14 minutes after sunset: 13.5 minutes for walking ¾ of a mil at 18 minutes per mil, plus 30 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi. In Jerusalem, the sun is 3.8° below geometric zenith around the equinox / equilux at this time.

The 49 amos component would be 26.46 seconds at the pace of an 18-minute mil, rounded to 30 seconds.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point8Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point8Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 4 Point 42 Degrees
items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, defined by the sun being 4.42° below the western horizon.

This is based on ¾ of a mil using a 22.5-minute mil, which is 16 ⅞ minutes.

This is a very early zman and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

This does not include the 33.07 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of a 22.5-minute mil.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh lists 4.37°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point42Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point42Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 4 Point 66 Degrees
items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, defined by the sun being 4.66° below the western horizon.

This is based on ¾ of a mil using a 24-minute mil, which is 18 minutes.

This is a very early zman and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

This does not include the 35.28 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of a 24-minute mil.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.
