Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point66Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 4 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, defined by the sun being 4.8° below the western horizon.

This corresponds to 18.6 minutes after sunset: ¾ of a 24-minute mil, plus 0.6 minutes for walking 49 amos for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi. The calculation is associated with Rav Yechiel Michel Shlezinger's Aizehu Bain Hashmashos and Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's calculations in Zmanei Hayom BaHalacha.

At this point, 3 medium-sized stars are visible to a non-expert with good vision with effort. An expert who knows where to look can see 3 medium stars as early as 15 minutes after sunset.

Hazmanim Bahalacha is cited as explaining that medium-sized stars would be visible as early as 14 minutes after sunset to an expert, based on 13.5 minutes for ¾ of an 18-minute mil plus 0.5 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

This is an early zman for tzais and should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 5 Point 95 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, defined by the sun being 5.95° below geometric zenith.

This is based on the sun’s position 24 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux. The 24 minutes is based on the Baal Hatanya’s calculation of 18 minutes, which is ¾ of a 24-minute mil, plus 4 minutes for shkiah amitis, plus 2 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

Sources cited include Hazmanim Bahalacha, Yisrael Vehazmanim Vol III, ch. 13, no. 53, p. 1026, and Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's calculations in Jewish Chrononomy and other sources.

Calculations show that the time is closer to 5.93° and was seemingly rounded to 5.95°.

Chabad calendars usually use a 6°-based zman that is built on this same calculation.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh lists 5.88°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 6 Point 45 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, based on the calculation of Rabbi Yechiel Michel Tucazinsky, defined by the sun being 6.45° below geometric zenith.

This is described as the sun’s position no later than 31 minutes after sea-level sunset in Jerusalem. At the height of the summer solstice, calculations show that 30.75 minutes after shkiah corresponds to 6.45° below geometric zenith. Around the equinox / equilux, 6.45° is 26.5 minutes after shkiah.

This calculation is found in Birur Halacha Yoreh Deah 262 and is described as the commonly used zman in Israel.

It is also used in Luach Itim Lebinah.

The raw source notes that this differs from the 6.1° or 6.2° calculation for Rabbi Tucazinsky’s time as calculated by Hazmanim Bahalacha.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 7 Point 083 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, defined by the sun being 7.083°, also written as 7° 5′, below the western horizon.

This is based on the observation of 3 medium-sized stars by Dr. Baruch Berthold Cohn in his luach Tabellen enthaltend die Zeitangaben für den Beginn der Nacht und des Tages für die Breitengrade + 66 bis -38, published in Strasbourg in 1899.

This calendar was very popular in Europe, and many other calendars based their tzais time on it.

Rav Dovid Tzvi Hoffman, in שו״ת מלמד להועיל, agreed to this zman in an exchange of letters with Baruch Cohn in Orach Chaim 30, as did the שו״ת בני ציון and the תנובת שדה.

It is very close to the time of the מקור חסד of the Sefer chasidim.

It is close to the sun’s position 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, but not exact. The actual position 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem is 7.205° at the equilux and 7.199° at the equinox.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not get low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the zman may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point083Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point083Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 7 Point 67 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of the Geonim. It is described as 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York, when twilight is longest. Sources mentioned include Igros Moshe Even Haezer 4, Ch. 4, Rabbi Heber's Shaarei Zmanim, chapter 10 page 87, chapter 12 page 108, Rabbi Simcha Bunim Cohen's The radiance of Shabbos, Divrei Shalom Vol. III, chapter 75, and Bais Av"i Vol. III, chapter 117.

It is calculated as the time when the sun is 7.67° below the western horizon.

Because this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to it.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point67Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point67Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 9 Point 3 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the calculations used in the Luach Itim Lebinah as the stringent time for tzais.

It is calculated as the time when the sun is 9.3° below the western horizon.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Tzais Geonim 9 Point 75 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of the Geonim. It is described as 60 minutes after sunset around the equinox / equilux, the day that a solar hour is 60 minutes in New York. This is the opinion of Rabbi Eliyahu Henkin and also follows the opinion of Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky.

It is calculated as the time when the sun is 9.75° below the western horizon.

Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

items.title: Tzais Geonim 8 Point 5 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, when the sun is 8.5° below the geometric horizon after sunset. Rabbi Meir Posen in the Ohr Meir calculated that at this point 3 small stars are visible, which is later than the required 3 medium stars.

This calculation is based on the sun’s position below the horizon 36 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which is 8.5° below the geometric zenith.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details
